lesson.title: Stationarity
lesson.body: A time series is strictly stationary if the joint distribution of any collection of observations is invariant to time shifts. The practically useful weaker condition is covariance (weak) stationarity: the mean E[x_t] = μ is constant, the autocovariance γ(h) = Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t) depends only on the lag h and not on t, and the variance γ(0) is finite. The autocorrelation function (ACF) is ρ(h) = γ(h)/γ(0), which normalises autocovariance to [-1, 1]. Non-stationary series with trends or time-varying variance violate these conditions and require transformation (differencing, log) before standard ARMA modelling.

rooms.question: A weakly stationary process requires which of the following?
steps: List the three conditions: mean, autocovariance, variance.
answers: Constant mean, autocovariance depending only on lag h, finite variance
Normal marginal distribution at every time point
Autocovariance equal to zero for all lags h > 0
Constant mean and strictly increasing variance
rooms.explanation: Weak (covariance) stationarity requires: (1) E[x_t] = μ for all t, (2) Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t) = γ(h) depends only on h, and (3) γ(0) < ∞. Normality is not required; that would be Gaussian stationarity.

rooms.question: The autocovariance at lag h is defined as γ(h) = Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t). What is γ(0)?
answers: Var(x_t), the variance of the series
0, because a variable is uncorrelated with itself at lag 0
ρ(0), the autocorrelation at lag 0
The mean μ of the series
rooms.explanation: γ(0) = Cov(x_t, x_t) = Var(x_t). It is the variance of the series. ρ(0) = γ(0)/γ(0) = 1 by definition, but γ(0) itself is the variance, not 1.

rooms.question: For a weakly stationary series, the ACF is ρ(h) = γ(h)/γ(0). Which property must ρ always satisfy?
steps: Consider the symmetry property: γ(h) = γ(-h).
answers: ρ(h) = ρ(-h) (the ACF is symmetric around lag 0)
ρ(h) is strictly decreasing as h increases
ρ(h) ≥ 0 for all h
ρ(h) → ∞ as h → ∞
rooms.explanation: By definition γ(h) = Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t) = Cov(x_t, x_{t+h}) = γ(-h), so ρ(h) = ρ(-h). The ACF is not required to be positive or monotonically decreasing; it can oscillate and can be negative.

rooms.question: Order these series from most stationary to least stationary (i.e., from requiring the fewest transformations to achieve stationarity):
items: I(1) random walk — needs first differencing
rooms.explanation: White noise is already stationary and needs nothing. A trend-stationary series needs only detrending. An I(1) random walk needs one difference. An I(2) series needs two differences and is the furthest from stationarity.

rooms.question: A random walk x_t = x_{t-1} + ε_t with ε_t ~ WN(0, σ²) and x_0 = 0. What is Var(x_t)?
steps: Unroll: x_t = ε_t + ε_{t-1} + ... + ε_1. Use independence of ε_i.
answers: tσ², which grows without bound
σ², constant
0, because increments are white noise
σ²/t, decreasing in t
rooms.explanation: x_t = Σ_{i=1}^{t} ε_i. Since ε_i are uncorrelated, Var(x_t) = tσ². This grows without bound, violating the constant variance requirement of stationarity. This is why a random walk is non-stationary.

boss.name: The Non-Stationary Phantom
boss.taunt: My mean drifts, my variance explodes. No model can tame a process with no fixed anchor in time.

questions.question: Which of the following processes is weakly stationary?
answers: x_t = x_{t-1} + ε_t (random walk)
x_t = t + ε_t (deterministic linear trend plus noise)
x_t = t·ε_t (time-scaled white noise)
questions.explanation: An AR(1) with |φ| < 1 is covariance stationary: its mean is zero, variance is σ²/(1−φ²) (finite and constant), and autocovariance depends only on lag. The random walk has variance tσ² (non-constant). The trend model has E[x_t] = t (non-constant mean). The scaled noise has variance t²σ² (non-constant).

questions.question: For a stationary process, γ(h) = Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t). Which statement about the autocovariance function is FALSE?
answers: γ(h) must be positive for all h > 0
γ(0) ≥ |γ(h)| for all h
γ(h) = γ(-h) for all h
γ(0) equals the variance of the process
questions.explanation: γ(h) can be negative — an oscillating series (e.g., AR(1) with φ < 0) has negative autocovariances at odd lags. The true statements: γ(0) ≥ |γ(h)| (by Cauchy-Schwarz), γ is symmetric, and γ(0) = Var(x_t).

questions.question: A series has constant mean but its variance doubles every year. Is it weakly stationary?
answers: No — weak stationarity requires constant variance (finite γ(0) independent of t)
Yes — constant mean is the only requirement for weak stationarity
Yes — if the mean is constant the series can be made stationary by standardisation
No — but only because the autocovariance is not defined
questions.explanation: Weak stationarity requires BOTH a constant mean AND that γ(h) = Cov(x_{t+h}, x_t) depends only on h (not t). If variance grows over time, γ(0, t) = Var(x_t) depends on t, violating the second condition. Constant mean alone is not sufficient.
